Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/openai-codex-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-gx36rd7w...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-gx36rd7w
Analyzing openai-codex-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-gx36rd7w) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines sources that fetch pre-built binaries from the official GitHub releases of the openai/codex repository, specifically for x86_64 and aarch64 architectures. The URLs use the `pkgver` variable, which is statically set to `0.141.0`, and no command substitution or dynamic data fetching occurs in the `source` entries. The `sha256sums` are provided for both architectures, though only one is used depending on the system; the aarch64 checksum is missing but this affects integrity checking, not safety for this gate.

The top-level code in the PKGBUILD consists only of variable assignments with no executable commands outside of the `package()` function. The `pkgver()` function is not defined, so version computation is static. There is no code executed during sourcing that would download or run untrusted payloads, exfiltrate data, or perform suspicious operations. Command substitutions or unsafe variable expansions are absent.

The `package()` function is not executed during `makepkg --noprepare --nobuild`, so its content (e.g., running the downloaded `codex` binary to generate completions) is out of scope for this safety gate. Fetching sources and sourcing the PKGBUILD in this context is safe and consistent with standard AUR packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>
No unsafe operations during source fetch and parse</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No unsafe operations during source fetch and parse[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches prebuilt binaries from the official GitHub repository of the OpenAI Codex project (https://github.com/openai/codex) for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures. The source URLs point directly to release binaries under the expected tag format (`rust-v${pkgver}`), which is consistent with the declared `pkgver`. Checksums are provided for both architectures, ensuring integrity verification.

The `package()` function installs the binary to `/usr/bin/codex` and generates shell completion scripts (bash, zsh, fish, etc.) by executing the installed binary with appropriate subcommands. This is a standard practice for CLI tools that support completion generation and does not involve any external downloads, code execution from untrusted sources, or obfuscated behavior. All operations occur within the build environment and target the package's own files.

There are no suspicious network operations, no use of dangerous constructs like `eval`, `curl|bash`, or data exfiltration attempts. The script adheres to standard AUR packaging practices and shows no signs of supply-chain compromise.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AUR packaging; no malicious behavior detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only variable definitions and a `package()` function. The source arrays point to legitimate upstream GitHub release tarballs with SHA256 checksums. There is no top-level code execution (no command substitutions, no `curl`/`wget` calls, no `pkgver()` function). The `makepkg` commands for this narrow gate only fetch the sources and source the PKGBUILD globals — both operations are safe. The `package()` function will be audited separately later.
</details>
<summary>Safe: standard source URLs, no top-level execution.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe: standard source URLs, no top-level execution.[/]

<security_assessment>

<details>
The PKGBUILD is clean and follows standard AUR packaging practices. The source tarballs are fetched from the official OpenAI GitHub releases (`https://github.com/openai/codex/releases/download/...`) with pinned SHA-256 checksums. The `package()` function only installs the prebuilt binary and generates shell completions by invoking that binary—a routine and expected step for CLI tools. There is no evidence of obfuscated code, suspicious network requests, data exfiltration, or any behavior that deviates from legitimate packaging.
</details>
<summary>Standard PKGBUILD from official source; no security issues found.</summary>
</security_assessment>

data.reasoning: Both models correctly identified the PKGBUILD as safe: sources official GitHub releases with pinned checksums, no top-level code execution, and standard package() function generating completions via the binary.
coverage_issues: Both models reviewed only PKGBUILD. No other files exist in the package (confirmed by makepkg --printsrcinfo output), so no critical files were missed.
learnings: Improve file selection logic: Models should explicitly note absence of .install/hook files when none exist.
Enhance prompt clarity: Emphasize that -bin packages require source URL/checksum scrutiny and binary execution in package() is normal.
Optimize cost: Both models spent excessive tokens on simple PKGBUILD; lighter models may suffice for straightforward packages.
